content.text: GitHub is the most widely used platform for hosting Git repositories. It adds collaboration features on top of Git: Pull Requests (code review), Issues (task tracking), Actions (CI/CD), Packages (private registry), and Pages (static hosting). Most Angular projects use GitHub as their primary collaboration hub.

attrs.alt: GitHub collaboration workflow: local machine, origin remote, PR, CI, merge

content.text: A remote is a named reference to another copy of the repository. The primary remote is conventionally named
. When you clone a repository, git automatically sets origin to the URL you cloned from.

git remote -v                                # list remotes and URLs
git remote add origin https://github.com/org/repo.git
git remote rename origin upstream             # rename
git remote remove upstream                   # delete
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:org/repo.git  # change URL
git remote show origin                        # detailed info

git fetch origin          # download all remote refs; no merge
git fetch origin main      # download only main
git pull                   # fetch + merge current branch's upstream
git pull --rebase          # fetch + rebase instead of merge
git pull origin main       # fetch and merge origin/main into current branch
git fetch + git rebase > git pull:
Fetch first so you can inspect what changed before merging. Use --rebase to avoid unnecessary merge commits on feature branches.
GitHub removed password authentication in August 2021. HTTPS clones now require a Personal Access Token (PAT) in place of a password.

# First-time: clone with HTTPS
git clone https://github.com/myorg/my-angular-app.git
# Enter: username = your GitHub username
# Enter: password = your PAT (not your account password)

# Save credentials so you aren't prompted every time
git config --global credential.helper store       # plaintext (~/.git-credentials)
git config --global credential.helper osxkeychain # macOS Keychain
git config --global credential.helper manager     # Windows Credential Manager
SSH authentication is more secure than HTTPS/PAT and doesn't require entering credentials on every push.

# Generate an SSH key pair
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@example.com"

# Add the public key to GitHub: Settings > SSH and GPG keys > New SSH key
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub   # copy this to GitHub

# Test the connection
ssh -T git@github.com
# Hi username! You've successfully authenticated...

# Clone using SSH
git clone git@github.com:myorg/my-angular-app.git

# Switch existing HTTPS remote to SSH
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:myorg/my-angular-app.git

A Pull Request (PR) is a structured request to merge one branch into another. It is the central collaboration mechanism on GitHub — where code review, discussion, and CI results converge before merging.
Push your feature branch to GitHub.
GitHub shows a 'Compare & pull request' button — click it.
Write a clear title and description using your team's PR template.
Set reviewers, labels, milestone, and linked issue.
Explain the WHY (what problem does this solve?) not just the what.
Link the related issue: 'Closes #42' auto-closes the issue on merge.
Include screenshots or recordings for UI changes.
List known limitations or follow-up tickets.
Review within 24 hours. Be specific — reference line numbers. Distinguish blocking vs. nit comments.
Respond to every comment. Push fix commits instead of force-pushing to keep review history.
Resolve discussions explicitly. Squash 'addressed' commits before merge.
GitHub Actions automates testing, building, and deploying Angular applications on every push and PR.

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: Angular CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, develop]

jobs:
  build-and-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Unit tests
        run: npm test -- --no-watch --no-progress --browsers=ChromeHeadless

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build -- --configuration production

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy to GitHub Pages

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build -- --base-href /my-angular-app/ --configuration production
      - name: Deploy
        uses: peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v4
        with:
          github_token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          publish_dir: ./dist/my-angular-app/browser
Protect your main branch in GitHub: Settings > Branches > Add rule.
Require a pull request before merging
CI must be green before merge
Require branches to be up to date
Do not allow bypassing the above
For open-source Angular libraries you don't own, the workflow is fork-based:
Fork the repository on GitHub (creates your personal copy).
Create a feature branch from upstream/main.
Push to your fork and open a PR against the upstream repository.
git fetch upstream; git rebase upstream/main
CLI lets you interact with GitHub from the terminal — create PRs, view CI status, manage issues — without opening a browser.

# Authenticate
gh auth login

# Create a PR from the current branch
gh pr create --title "feat(auth): add JWT refresh" --body "Closes #42"

# View PR status and CI checks
gh pr status
gh pr checks

# Merge a PR
gh pr merge --squash

# Clone a repo
gh repo clone myorg/my-angular-app

# View issues
gh issue list
gh issue create --title "Bug: null pointer in ProfileComponent"
GitHub Issues are the canonical task tracker for Angular projects. Best practices:
One issue per bug/feature. Link it to a PR with 'Closes #N'.
Use labels: bug, feature, documentation, good first issue, breaking change.
Use milestones to group issues into a release.
GitHub Projects (board view) gives a Kanban-style overview across issues and PRs.

# Optimised caching strategy for large Angular projects
jobs:
  install:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      cache-key: ${{ steps.cache.outputs.cache-hit }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci

  lint:
    needs: install
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci  # cache hit — nearly instant
      - run: npm run lint
Matrix Testing Against Multiple Node Versions

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        node-version: [18, 20, 22]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test -- --no-watch --browsers=ChromeHeadless

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build -- --configuration production
      # deploy to hosting...
Never hard-code API keys or tokens in Angular source code. GitHub Secrets stores encrypted values accessible only to Actions workflows.

# Setting a secret: GitHub repo > Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions > New secret
# Name: API_URL
# Value: https://api.example.com

# Using it in a workflow
- name: Build with API URL
  run: npm run build -- --configuration production
  env:
    API_URL: ${{ secrets.API_URL }}
For runtime Angular environment variables, inject them at build time via a custom script that generates environment.ts from secrets:
Host private Angular libraries in GitHub Packages — scoped to your GitHub organisation.

# GitHub Actions — publish on tag push
on:
  push:
    tags:
      - 'v*'

jobs:
  publish:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      packages: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          registry-url: 'https://npm.pkg.github.com'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: ng build ui-components --configuration production
      - run: npm publish --workspace=packages/ui-components
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
file defines which team members are automatically requested as reviewers for changes to specific paths.

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Global owners — required reviewers for all PRs
*                   @myorg/platform-team

# Frontend team owns Angular components and styles
/src/app/           @myorg/frontend-team

# Infrastructure team owns CI and deployment config
/.github/           @myorg/infra-team

# Security team reviews auth code
/src/app/auth/      @myorg/security-team
A PR template pre-fills the PR description with a checklist. Create it at

## Summary
<!-- What does this PR do? Link the related issue -->
Closes #

## Changes
- [ ] New component/service
- [ ] Updated existing component
- [ ] Bug fix

## Testing
- [ ] Unit tests added/updated
- [ ] Tested manually in browser
- [ ] No regressions in existing tests

## Checklist
- [ ] PR title follows Conventional Commits format
- [ ] No console.log left in code
- [ ] Breaking changes documented
GitHub webhooks fire HTTP POST requests to external URLs on repository events — push, PR open, PR merged, deployment. Angular projects use webhooks to:
Trigger deployment pipelines (Vercel, Netlify, AWS Amplify) on main push.
Post PR review reminders to Slack.
Sync issues to project management tools (Jira, Linear).
Invalidate CDN caches after a production deploy.
Enable 'Require signed commits' — all commits must be GPG-signed.
Enable secret scanning — GitHub alerts you when a key is accidentally committed.
Enable Dependabot — auto-creates PRs for dependency security updates.
Set up CODEOWNERS to require domain expert review.
Enable required status checks — CI must pass before merge.
Use branch protection for main/develop; consider also protecting release/* branches.
GitHub extends git with collaboration, review, and automation tools that are essential for professional Angular development.
Pull Requests are the gate for all changes to main — code review + CI must pass.
, lint, test, and build on every PR.
Branch protection rules enforce PR workflow — disable direct pushes to main.
CLI automates PR creation, status checks, and merging from the terminal.
